title: Difference of squares

confidence.label: Grounded
confidence.meaning: symbolically proven to follow from the previous step

steps.operation: Factor the expression using the difference of squares formula
steps.justification: The difference of squares formula states that for any two terms $x$ and $y$, $x^2 - y^2 = (x - y) \cdot (x + y)$.

confidence.label: Grounded
confidence.meaning: symbolically proven to follow from the previous step
confidence.reason: symbolically equivalent to the previous step

overall_confidence.label: Grounded
overall_confidence.meaning: symbolically proven to follow from the previous step

__power_2.name: a^{2}
__power_2.description: $a^{2}$ represents the square of the variable $a$.

a.name: a
a.description: $a$ is the first variable or term being squared in the difference of squares expression.

__power_4.name: b^{2}
__power_4.description: $b^{2}$ represents the square of the variable $b$.

b.name: b
b.description: $b$ is the second variable or term being squared in the difference of squares expression.

goal: This derivation demonstrates how to factor the difference of two squares, $a^2 - b^2$, into the product of two binomials, $(a-b)(a+b)$.
followups: Can you show how to derive the difference of squares formula?
What are some other common algebraic factorization formulas?
How is the difference of squares used in solving equations?
